next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 14

By dusk the camp had learned a new way to be polite.

Nobody told Mina to stay clear of the kitchen. Nobody had to. The main house carried sound too well for that kind of order to survive as an order; it would have become gossip before the sentence was finished. Instead the place had gone careful around her. A pot of rice sat on the stove with the lid tipped so steam could get out. Two fillets of jack were wrapped in foil on a tray, one gone, one waiting. Someone had moved her mug from the drainboard to the far end of the counter, beside the can of instant coffee nobody claimed unless they were furious or broke. Through the screen wall the spoonbills kept up their raw nesting racket in the darkening trees, and under that noise the stilts complained every time the wind leaned.

The argument from the night before had not stopped ringing just because everybody had run out of things to throw at it.

Tomas's silence had been the loudest part. He had not shouted after the first few ugly minutes. He had gotten worse than that. He had gone exact. He had named fuel, hours, and the shape of his own suspension as if he were reading his losses off a county form. He had named his body too, which had been harder to hear. Mina had stood there with the annex logbook on the table between them and understood too late that proof could strip skin faster than lies did. Rhea had not rescued her. Rhea had also not let Tomas turn the whole thing into a neat charge sheet with Mina as sole defendant. That had been its own kind of mercy, and it had not felt kind.

All day Mina had been...
open_hooks_to_carry: Mina shares the annex evidence and destroys the easy redemption narrative.
Imogen argues for publicity, Tomas argues from lost wages and humiliation, and Rhea refuses everyone's preferred simplification.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ng at a half-corrupted hydrophone file and seeing possible rescue.

Mina put one hand on the bottom rung of the ladder to the boardwalk and stopped there.

She had wanted the eel to be clean in at least one direction. Maybe not innocent, because innocence had been gone for chapters now and she was not stupid enough to ask the creek for it. But she had wanted it to be clean as evidence and clean as correction, a thing that could be real and beautiful and wild enough, or at least not provably hers enough, to lever her life a few inches away from disgrace. She had wanted discovery to do what confession never did: rearrange blame into something survivable.

The logbook killed that fantasy on the page where live material entered outgoing flow.

The badge killed the rest.

What the spoonbills stirred above at mating peak, what had rolled blue under the blackwater and made donors hungry and scientists alert and her own blood go hot with need, was not a miracle delivered intact from nature to witness. It was a contaminated inheritance. It had come through concrete, storm surge, emptied tanks, men protecting legal exposure, and her own cheap yearning to be let past a door. The creek had taken all of it and made a living line anyway. That did not redeem the making. It only made the truth harder to touch without wanting to use it.

She climbed to the boardwalk with the dry sack dragging at her shoulder and the recovered badge knocking lightly against the logbook inside, plastic tapping paper like a metronome for a story she could no longer tell cleanly to anyone, least of all herself.
state_deltas_required: Competing desires to protect the eel, save the camp, save a marriage, and escape blame
The three-way relationship and the eel project are stripped of romance and revealed as entangled with use, class, and extraction.
